body:
**Constraints:**
- Use only one sub-structure.

**Goal:**
- Build a rocket capable of lifting off from the ground and ascending into the sky in the simulation environment.

**Evaluation Protocol:**
- The rocket will be placed at position (x=0, y=0, z=0) on the ground plane.
- During the simulation, the firing control key of the rocket engine will be pressed and held continuously.
- The motion trajectory of the rocket will be recorded throughout the simulation.

**Scoring Metrics:**
- *Maximum Height:* The highest vertical position (z) reached by any block of the rocket (higher is better).
- *Trajectory Deviation:* The average lateral distance between the rocket’s actual trajectory and the ideal vertical line (smaller is better).
- *Maximum Speed:* The highest speed achieved by any block of the rocket (higher is better).
- *Cost:* The total number of blocks used to construct the rocket (fewer is better).

title: planner message
summary: <building_plan> <overall_structure> <description> The overall structure is a rocket consisting of a central vertical column built on the starting stone block, with two small wooden blocks stacked on top to provide heigh…
body:
<building_plan>
  <overall_structure>
    <description>
      The overall structure is a rocket consisting of a central vertical column built on the starting stone block, with two small wooden blocks stacked on top to provide height. At the top level (z=2 to 3), four horizontal arms extend outward in the +x, -x, +y, and -y directions. Each arm consists of two small wooden blocks placed side by side horizontally. A water cannon is attached to the bottom face of the outer wooden block of each arm, oriented to point downward (-z direction) for downward spray and upward recoil. A torch is attached to the east (or equivalent outward) side face of the inner wooden block of each arm, oriented to point downward, with its flame positioned to heat the corresponding water cannon, enabling steam mode for increased thrust. The design ensures symmetry for stable vertical ascent, with engine outlets above the ground plane to avoid conflicts.
    </description>
    <functionality>
      When the firing control key is pressed and held, all four water cannons activate simultaneously, spraying steam downward due to heating from the torches, generating upward recoil force. The total thrust exceeds the structure's weight, causing the rocket to lift off and accelerate upward. The symmetric placement of the engines ensures the thrust vector passes through the center of mass, minimizing lateral deviation and promoting a straight vertical trajectory. Continuous firing results in sustained acceleration, achieving high maximum height and speed.
    </functionality>
    <assembly>
      N/A - The rocket is constructed as a single sub-structure with all components directly attached.
    </assembly>
    <motion_control>
      The structure has no moving parts beyond the water cannons' firing mechanism. All water cannons are controlled by a single firing key, which is pressed and held continuously during simulation. The expected motion is a stable vertical ascent along the +z axis, with minimal lateral deviation due to symmetric thrust. The rocket lifts off from the ground and accelerates upward indefinitely under constant thrust, subject to simulation constraints.
    </motion_control>
  </overall_structure>

  <sub_structures>
    <sub_structure_1>
      <name>Rocket</name>
      <description>The sub-structure is a symmetric rocket with a central column and four extending arms at the top, each arm supporting a steam-powered engine (water cannon heated by a torch) oriented downward for upward thrust.</description>
      <components>1 starting stone block, 10 small wooden blocks, 4 water cannons, 4 torches.</components>
      <assembly>N/A - Single sub-structure.</assembly>
      <motion_control>The water cannons are fired continuously via the control key, producing upward recoil for vertical ascent. No additional controls are needed; the design relies on symmetry for straight-line motion.</motion_control>
      <function>This sub-structure serves as the complete rocket, providing sufficient symmetric thrust to achieve lift-off, high altitude, high speed, and minimal trajectory deviation while using a reasonable number of blocks.</function>
      <design_requirements>The structure must be symmetric in the x-y plane to minimize deviation. Water cannons must be oriented downward with outlets above z=0. Torches must be positioned such that their heating spheres overlap with the water cannons to enable steam mode, without the flames being extinguished by steam plumes or causing physical overlaps. Positions may be micro-adjusted in later stages to resolve conflicts based on actual build execution.</design_requirements>
    </sub_structure_1>
  </sub_structures>
</building_plan>

body:
Let's proceed step by step as per the requirements: structural placement (collision, attachment, minimum distance), then functional validation, then physical feasibility.

---
## STRUCTURAL CHECK (Step-by-step, block-by-block)

### Block 1: Small Wooden Block (base of central column)
- Position: [0, 0, 0] (base block, replaces the default starting block)
- No previous blocks; this is the origin.

---

### Block 2: Small Wooden Block (central column)
- Attachment: Top face of block 1 ([0, 0, 1])
- Center: [0, 0, 1]
- Distance to block 1: |[0, 0, 1] - [0, 0, 0]| = 1 (along z)
    - Both blocks are [1,1,1], so minimum required: 1 (half-height + half-height = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1)
    - PASS: No overlap.

---

### Block 3: Small Wooden Block (top of central column)
- Attachment: Top face of block 2 ([0, 0, 2])
- Center: [0, 0, 2]
- Distances:
    - to block 2: |[0,0,2] - [0,0,1]| = 1 (z) → PASS
    - to block 1: |[0,0,2] - [0,0,0]| = 2 (z) → > 1, PASS

---

### Block 4: Small Wooden Block (east arm base)
- Attachment: East face of block 3 ([1, 0, 2])
- Center: [1, 0, 2]
- Distances:
    - to block 3: |[1,0,2] - [0,0,2]| = 1 (x) → PASS
    - to block 2: |[1,0,2] - [0,0,1]| = sqrt(1^2 + 0^2 + 1^2) = sqrt(2) ≈ 1.41 > 1, PASS
    - to block 1: |[1,0,2] - [0,0,0]| = sqrt(1^2 + 0^2 + 2^2) = sqrt(5) ≈ 2.24 > 1, PASS

---

### Block 5: Small Wooden Block (east arm downward extension)
- Attachment: Bottom face of block 4 ([1, 0, 1])
- Center: [1, 0, 1]
- Distances:
    - to block 4: |[1,0,1] - [1,0,2]| = 1 (z) → PASS
    - to block 3: |[1,0,1] - [0,0,2]| = sqrt(1^2 + 0^2 + 1^2) = 1.41 > 1, PASS
    - to block 2: |[1,0,1] - [0,0,1]| = 1 (x) → PASS
    - to block 1: |[1,0,1] - [0,0,0]| = sqrt(1^2 + 0^2 + 1^2) = 1.41 > 1, PASS

---

### Block 6: Water Cannon (east engine)
- Attachment: East face of block 5 ([2, 0, 1])
- Water Cannon shape: [1, 1.75, 1], attached at center of 1x1 face, offset 0.5 in x (so center at [2,0,1])
- Orientation: Downward (outlet below, for upward thrust)
- Distances:
    - to block 5: |[2,0,1] - [1,0,1]| = 1 (x) → PASS
    - to block 4: |[2,0,1] - [1,0,2]| = sqrt(1^2 + 0^2 + 1^2) = 1.41 > 1, PASS
    - to block 3: |[2,0,1] - [0,0,2]| = sqrt(2^2 + 0^2 + 1^2) = sqrt(5) ≈ 2.24 > 1, PASS
    - to all others: greater distances, PASS

---

### Block 7: Torch (heater for east water cannon)
- Attachment: East face of block 4 ([2, 0, 2]), oriented downward
- Torch shape: support 0.5 along x, vertical shaft 1 along -z, flame at [2, 0, 1]
- Center: [2, 0, 2]; flame at [2, 0, 1]
- Distances:
    - to block 4: |[2,0,2] - [1,0,2]| = 1 (x) → PASS
    - to block 6 (water cannon): |[2,0,2] - [2,0,1]| = 1 (z) → PASS (body-to-body), but flame at [2,0,1] overlaps with water cannon's center
    - Torch has no attachable faces, so no further attachments here.

    **Overlap check**: Water cannon center at [2,0,1], torch flame at [2,0,1]. This is coincident. Torch shaft is vertical, so the body of the torch occupies [2,0,2] to [2,0,1]. Water cannon body is [2,0,1] ±0.5 in x,z, ±0.875 in y. There is direct overlap at the flame and cannon center (possible collision). The torch's heating sphere will fully overlap the water cannon inlet and part of its body.

    **Micro-adjustment needed**: As per the note, offset the torch by 0.1 in y (say, [2,0.1,2] with flame at [2,0.1,1]). This moves the shaft slightly off the cannon center, avoiding collision but keeping the flame within 0.3 units of the water cannon's body (since the water cannon is 1 unit wide, and the flame is only 0.1 off-center).

    - New distances: |[2,0.1,1] - [2,0,1]| = 0.1 (y) < 0.5 (half-width), so still within heating range.
    - No overlap of solid bodies if the torch shaft is not intersecting the cannon's body.

---

### Block 8: Small Wooden Block (west arm base)
- Attachment: West face of block 3 ([-1, 0, 2])
- Center: [-1, 0, 2]
- Distances:
    - to block 3: |[-1,0,2] - [0,0,2]| = 1 (x) → PASS
    - to all others: distances > 1, PASS

---

### Block 9: Small Wooden Block (west arm downward extension)
- Attachment: Bottom face of block 8 ([-1, 0, 1])
- Center: [-1, 0, 1]
- Distances:
    - to block 8: |[-1,0,1] - [-1,0,2]| = 1 (z) → PASS

---

### Block 10: Water Cannon (west engine)
- Attachment: West face of block 9 ([-2, 0, 1])
- Center: [-2, 0, 1]
- Distances:
    - to block 9: |[-2,0,1] - [-1,0,1]| = 1 (x) → PASS

---

### Block 11: Torch (heater for west water cannon)
- Attachment: West face of block 8 ([-2, 0, 2]), oriented downward
- Torch at [-2, 0, 2], flame at [-2, 0, 1]
- As before, micro-adjust to [-2, 0.1, 2], flame at [-2, 0.1, 1]
- Distances:
    - to block 10 (water cannon): |[-2,0.1,1] - [-2,0,1]| = 0.1 (y) < 0.5, within heating range, no solid overlap

---

### Block 12: Small Wooden Block (north arm base)
- Attachment: North face of block 3 ([0, 1, 2])
- Center: [0, 1, 2]
- Distances:
    - to block 3: |[0,1,2] - [0,0,2]| = 1 (y) → PASS

---

### Block 13: Small Wooden Block (north arm downward extension)
- Attachment: Bottom face of block 12 ([0, 1, 1])
- Center: [0, 1, 1]
- Distances:
    - to block 12: |[0,1,1] - [0,1,2]| = 1 (z) → PASS

---

### Block 14: Water Cannon (north engine)
- Attachment: North face of block 13 ([0, 2, 1])
- Center: [0, 2, 1]
- Distances:
    - to block 13: |[0,2,1] - [0,1,1]| = 1 (y) → PASS

---

### Block 15: Torch (heater for north water cannon)
- Attachment: North face of block 12 ([0, 2, 2]), oriented downward
- Torch at [0, 2, 2], flame at [0, 2, 1]
- Micro-adjust to [0, 2, 2.1], flame at [0, 2, 1.1]
- Distance to block 14: |[0,2,1.1] - [0,2,1]| = 0.1 (z) < 0.5, within heating range, no solid overlap

---

### Block 16: Small Wooden Block (south arm base)
- Attachment: South face of block 3 ([0, -1, 2])
- Center: [0, -1, 2]
- Distances:
    - to block 3: |[0,-1,2] - [0,0,2]| = 1 (y) → PASS

---

### Block 17: Small Wooden Block (south arm downward extension)
- Attachment: Bottom face of block 16 ([0, -1, 1])
- Center: [0, -1, 1]
- Distances:
    - to block 16: |[0,-1,1] - [0,-1,2]| = 1 (z) → PASS

---

### Block 18: Water Cannon (south engine)
- Attachment: South face of block 17 ([0, -2, 1])
- Center: [0, -2, 1]
- Distances:
    - to block 17: |[0,-2,1] - [0,-1,1]| = 1 (y) → PASS

---

### Block 19: Torch (heater for south water cannon)
- Attachment: South face of block 16 ([0, -2, 2]), oriented downward
- Torch at [0, -2, 2], flame at [0, -2, 1]
- Micro-adjust to [0, -2, 2.1], flame at [0, -2, 1.1]
- Distance to block 18: |[0,-2,1.1] - [0,-2,1]| = 0.1 (z) < 0.5, within heating range, no solid overlap

---

**Summary of Structural Check:**  
- All blocks are attached to available faces, with no overlaps or minimum distance violations.
- Micro-adjustments for torch positions (by 0.1–0.2 units in y or z) avoid physical overlap with water cannons while maintaining heating coverage.
- All placements maintain symmetry in the x-y plane.

---

## FUNCTIONAL VALIDATION

1. **Symmetry:**  
   - The structure is symmetric in the x-y plane. The four arms are evenly spaced.

2. **Water Cannons (Steam Engines):**  
   - All four water cannons are oriented downward (for upward thrust).  
   - Each is attached to the end of a vertical arm, with its outlet above z=0 (since the base block is at z=0, and all engines are at z=1).  
   - Each engine is individually controllable.

3. **Torches (Heaters):**  
   - Each torch is positioned such that its flame (heating sphere, radius 0.3) overlaps with the body of the corresponding water cannon.  
   - Micro-adjustments ensure the torch shaft does not collide with the water cannon, and the flame is within heating distance.
   - Torches are not in the direct path of steam plumes (since the torch body is offset from the cannon outlet), so are unlikely to be extinguished during operation.

4. **No Power Transmission Components:**  
   - None required; water cannons and torches are self-contained.

5. **Control:**  
   - All water cannons are controlled by a single key for simultaneous firing, as specified.

---

## PHYSICAL FEASIBILITY

- **Gravity:**  
   - The structure is vertically symmetric and compact.  
   - The arms are supported by the central column and base; the mass is distributed evenly.  
   - The total mass (approx.):  
     - 10 small wooden blocks × 0.3 = 3.0  
     - 4 water cannons × 1.5 = 6.0  
     - 4 torches × 1 = 4.0  
     - Total: 13.0 units

- **Thrust:**  
   - Each water cannon in steam mode produces 1.6 × 8.6 = 13.76 units of force.
   - 4 cannons: 55.04 units of upward force, more than enough to lift the structure (assuming normal gravity, mass 13.0 units).

- **Mounting and Alignment:**  
   - All water cannons are rigidly attached to the ends of arms, with proper orientation.
   - Torches are attached to arm bases, not to the cannons themselves, and are physically separated by micro-adjustment.

---

# FINAL ASSESSMENT

- All blocks are properly placed, with no overlaps or minimum distance violations.
- All functional requirements (symmetry, heating for steam mode, thrust, control) are met.
- The design is physically feasible and should operate as intended.

**This blueprint fully meets the design requirements for the 'Rocket' sub-structure.**

`TERMINATE`

title: builder thought
summary: Hello! I'm your engineering building assistant, specialized in constructing functional structures in a simulated 3D environment. I'll help you build step by step based on your instructions or blueprints, using a set of…
body:
Hello! I'm your engineering building assistant, specialized in constructing functional structures in a simulated 3D environment. I'll help you build step by step based on your instructions or blueprints, using a set of specialized tools. We'll work collaboratively—you can provide guidance, and I'll suggest tools or actions as needed. Remember, I build one block at a time to ensure accuracy, and I can't make parallel tool calls in a single response.

Before we dive in, let me introduce all my available tools in detail. Each tool helps with specific aspects of building, modifying, or inspecting the structure. I'll describe what they accomplish, their key parameters, and what information I need from you (or the blueprint) to use them effectively. Note that many tools have an optional "note" argument—this is very important and useful! It allows us to add a conceptual description or identifier for the block/connection, making it easier to reference later (e.g., "base of central column" or "east steam engine"). This acts as a powerful label for clarity, especially in complex builds.

### Building and Attachment Tools:
- **start**: This initializes the machine by creating and positioning the very first block (the starting point of the structure). It's essential for beginning any build. Parameters: init_shift (a list of [x, y, z] offsets for position, default [0,0,0]); init_rotation (a list of [yaw, pitch, roll] in degrees for orientation, default [0,0,0]); note (optional description). To use it effectively, provide any custom positioning/rotation if needed; otherwise, defaults work for standard starts. Returns a status message.
  
- **attach_block_to**: Attaches a new block to a specific face of an existing block, expanding the structure. Available new block types: 'Powered Wheel', 'Small Wooden Block', 'Torch', 'Water Cannon'. Parameters: base_block (ID of the existing block, can be string or int); face (the capitalized letter label of the face to attach to, e.g., 'A' or 'B'—use get_block_details to check available faces); new_block (type from the list); note (optional but recommended for identification). I need the exact base block ID and face; if unsure, I'll query details first. Returns a status message. Great for sequential building.

- **connect_blocks**: Connects two existing blocks using a connector, for stability or functionality (e.g., bracing distant parts). Available connectors: 'Brace', 'Winch'. Parameters: block_a and block_b (IDs); face_a and face_b (faces to connect from/to); connector (type); note (optional description). Connections fail if faces are too close. Provide block IDs and faces; use get_block_details for face info. Returns a status message.

### Modification Tools:
- **remove_block**: Removes a specific block from the structure, useful for corrections. Parameters: block_id (string or int). Just need the ID; returns a status message.

- **flip_spin**: Flips the spin direction of a block (only works on spinnable blocks like wheels). Parameters: block_id. Provide the ID; returns a status message.

- **shift_block**: Adjusts a block's position by a small offset, helpful for fine-tuning after attachment (e.g., to avoid overlaps). Parameters: block_id; shift_real (list of [x, y, z] offsets, each in [-0.5, 0.5]). Specify the ID and precise offsets; too much shift can detach it.

- **twist_block**: Rotates a block clockwise relative to its attached surface, useful for reorienting (e.g., changing a pointer's direction). Parameters: block_id; angle (in degrees, e.g., 90, 180). Provide ID and angle; multiple twists can achieve desired orientation. Returns a status message.

### Inspection and Reset Tools:
- **get_block_details**: Retrieves full details of a specific block, including position, rotation, and face labels (crucial for knowing attachable faces, labeled with capitalized letters). Parameters: block_id. Just need the ID; returns a string with details. Use this before attachments or connections to confirm faces.

- **get_machine_summary**: Provides an overview of the entire current machine state (without face captions). No parameters. Returns a string summary. **Important**: I must use this for a final check before ending the build process—I'll always remind us to do so.

- **reset**: Clears the entire machine back to an empty state. No parameters. Useful for starting over.

The environment uses a global [x, y, z] coordinate system: +x east, +y north, +z up (sky). I'll follow your blueprint or instructions precisely, step by step, without inferring or changing details. If something's unclear, I'll ask for clarification. The "note" in tools like start, attach_block_to, and connect_blocks is key—use it to label blocks functionally (e.g., "east steam engine") for easy reference.

This seems like a hovering or propulsion device. Let's build it one block at a time, starting with block 1.

title: guidance message
summary: The current structure aligns with the blueprint's functional intent of a central column supporting four symmetric arms (east, west, north, south), each equipped with a downward-oriented Water Cannon heated by a Torch fo…
body:
The current structure aligns with the blueprint's functional intent of a central column supporting four symmetric arms (east, west, north, south), each equipped with a downward-oriented Water Cannon heated by a Torch for steam-mode upward recoil thrust. Adjustments were made by adding extra downward extension blocks (IDs 8, 12, 17, 22) to resolve physical overlaps between Torches and Water Cannons during construction, while preserving symmetry and ensuring heating spheres (centered at z=1 with radius 0.3) intersect the upper parts of the Water Cannons (e.g., near inlets at z=0.75) to activate steam mode. All components are correctly positioned and oriented as per the latest feedback: central column at [0,0,0] to [0,0,2]; east arm extending to Water Cannon at [2,0,0] downward, heated by Torch at [2,0,2] downward; west arm to [-2,0,0] downward, heated at [-2,0,2] downward; north arm to [0,2,0] downward, heated at [0,2,2] downward; south arm to [0,-2,0] downward, heated at [0,-2,2] downward. This configuration should function as a controllable hovering or thrusting device with enhanced steam recoil when heated.
